title: ISE Downloadable ACL (dACL) Push Failures and ACL-Length Truncation Detection

controlTest.positiveScenario: Push an oversized dACL to an old switch. UC-17.1.75 detects 5438 truncation.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Reasonable dACLs to current switches. No fires.

dataSources: ISE syslog `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` *Authorization* + *Failed-Attempts* — codes `5436` (dACL push successful), `5437` (dACL push failed), `5438` (dACL too long for switch). Field: `dacl_name`, `dacl_size_bytes`, `nas_ip`, `failure_reason`.
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (5437, 5438)
| stats count by dacl_name, nas_ip, MessageCode, failure_reason
| where count > 5
description: Detects: (a) dACLs that fail to push to switches (incompatible IOS, ACL syntax errors). (b) dACLs that are truncated by switches with limited TCAM (older Catalyst with shallow ACL TCAM).
value: Truncated dACLs silently weaken access control — the policy in ISE may allow X but the switch enforces Y. Catching dACL push failures lets policy engineering correct ACL design.
implementation: Schedule the search every 1 hour over the last 24 hours; trigger when dACL drift is observed; throttle by `dacl_name` for 24 hours.
visualization: dACL push failure trend; per-dACL failure heatmap.
knownFalsePositives:
(a) Intermittent push failures during NAD reboot.
(b) Older Catalyst 2960 series have known ACL-length limits.
mitreAttack: N/A (policy hygiene — measures dACL/AuthZ profile drift, no adversary technique)
grandmaExplanation: ISE pushes access rules to switches when a user connects. If the rule is too big or the switch can't accept it, the rule may be cut short — and the user gets less restricted than intended. We watch for these silent rule failures.
